source.title: Hydraulic Shock Safety Bulletin - Anhydrous Ammonia Release at Millard Refrigerated Services, Inc.

event.top_event: Loss of Containment - Anhydrous Ammonia Release
event.incident_type: Catastrophic piping failure due to hydraulic shock
actions_taken: Three Millard employees went to roof to manually close valves supplying ammonia to blast freezer
All other Millard employees evacuated to upwind location
Mobile Fire Department responded with hazmat technicians
U.S. Coast Guard halted water traffic on industrial canal
Mobile County Emergency Management advised shelter-in-place
Impromptu triage site established at gas station 2 miles away
Air monitoring conducted by MFD and U.S. Coast Guard
event.summary: On August 23, 2010, a hydraulic shock event caused catastrophic failure of a 12-inch roof-mounted suction pipe at Millard Refrigerated Services, releasing 32,100 pounds of anhydrous ammonia. The incident was triggered by a 7-hour power outage followed by manual interruption of a defrost cycle, which bypassed the programmed bleed sequence. When the system restarted, high-pressure hot gas mixed with low-temperature liquid in the suction line, causing condensation-induced shock and vapor-propelled liquid phenomena that ruptured both the roof piping and an evaporator coil. The ammonia cloud traveled 0.25 miles across Theodore Industrial Canal, exposing 153 people including 1 Millard employee, 9 ship crew members, and 143 offsite contractors. Of those exposed, 32 were hospitalized and 4 placed in intensive care.
recommendations: Avoid grouping multiple large-capacity evaporators to a single set of control valves; use individual valve control groups for each evaporator
Program defrost control systems to automatically depressurize or bleed coils upon restart after power outage or interruption prior to opening suction stop valve
Avoid manual interruption of evaporators during defrost; equip control systems with password-protected controls to restrict access to trained and authorized personnel only
Ensure pump-out times are long enough to remove residual liquid refrigerant from evaporator coils prior to introducing hot gas, especially after low-load periods or power outages

In event of ammonia release that cannot be promptly isolated, activate emergency shut-down switch to de-energize pumps, compressors and valves instead of attempting to isolate leaking equipment while system is running

hazards.name: High-Pressure Hot Gas in Evaporator Coils
Low-Temperature Liquid Ammonia in Suction Lines

threats.name: Mixing of high-pressure hot gas with low-temperature liquid
Inadequate pump-out time leaving residual liquid in coils
Multiple evaporators grouped to single control valve set
Continued ammonia circulation after initial rupture

consequences.name: Rupture of evaporator coil in blast freezer
Release of 32,100 pounds of anhydrous ammonia
Formation of ammonia vapor cloud traveling 0.25 miles
Exposure of 153 people to ammonia vapor
Intensive care admission of 4 exposed workers
Contamination of 8 million pounds of poultry and packaging
Ammonia concentrations up to 7,275 ppm in blast freezer
Potential for deflagration if ignition source present

controls.name: Programmed defrost control sequence
controls.barrier_role: Prevent rapid depressurization and mixing of hot gas with cold liquid
controls.lod_basis: Designed to automatically execute proper defrost sequence including bleed cycle

supporting_text: the Millard refrigeration system operator manually cleared an alarm in the system. This resulted in an interruption of a defrost cycle that was in progress
the control system did not recognize that the blast freezer evaporator unit contained high-pressure hot gas when it allowed the suction stop valve to open
This manual bypass of the programmed defrost sequence allowed the low-temperature liquid and hot gas to mix in the same pipe

controls.name: Bleed cycle (pressure equalization valve)
controls.barrier_role: Depressurize evaporator coil before opening suction stop valve
controls.lod_basis: Designed to slowly depressurize coil after hot gas defrost to prevent rapid pressure drop

supporting_text: The evaporator switched directly from defrost mode into refrigeration mode without bleeding hot gas from the evaporator coil
The bleed phase is necessary to prevent a rapid reduction of pressure in the coil when the low-temperature suction stop valve opens

controls.name: Individual control valve groups per evaporator
controls.barrier_role: Limit volume of high-pressure gas released during suction valve opening
controls.lod_basis: Design practice to reduce energy stored in evaporator coils and limit shock magnitude

supporting_text: Millard and the designers of the refrigeration system designed the blast freezer evaporators at the Theodore facility with four evaporator coils grouped to one set of control valves

Designing the system with multiple evaporator coils controlled by a single valve arrangement reduced the overall number of valves that needed to be installed for each evaporator; therefore, reducing the initial system cost

Had Millard assigned each evaporator to an individual set of control valves, only 15 cubic feet at 70 psig would have been released to the header when the control system reset signaled the suction stop valve to open

controls.name: Adequate pump-out time in defrost cycle
controls.barrier_role: Remove residual liquid ammonia from evaporator coils before hot gas introduction
controls.lod_basis: Operational procedure to prevent liquid slugs that can cause hydraulic shock

supporting_text: According to Millard documentation, the blast freezer evaporator defrost timer was originally intended to run the pump-out cycle for 20 minutes; however CSB investigators reviewed control system data for the evaporators and found that it only lasted 15 minutes on the days leading up to the incident
the power outage, followed by a low-load period in the middle of the night, allowed for the low-temperature liquid ammonia to fill the coils and settle in the bottom evaporator
A longer pump-out time would have cleared a greater quantity of liquid refrigerant from the coil prior to the hot gas cycle

controls.name: Password-protected control system access
controls.barrier_role: Restrict manual override of defrost sequence to trained and authorized personnel
controls.lod_basis: Access control to prevent unauthorized or improper manual intervention in critical system sequences

supporting_text: This was enabled by a lack of restricted access to control system modifications. Had password-protected controls been in place, they could have been used to restrict access to only authorized personnel trained to modify the refrigeration system sequence

Avoid the manual interruption of evaporators while in defrost and equip control systems with password protected controls to ensure only trained and authorized personnel have the authority to manually override system processes

controls.name: Air monitoring equipment in freezers
controls.barrier_role: Detect high ammonia concentrations and trigger alarms
controls.lod_basis: Detection system to alert personnel to ammonia release inside facility

supporting_text: At approximately the same time, alarms sounded within the plant due to the detection of high concentrations of ammonia indoors by air-monitoring equipment in the freezers

controls.name: Emergency evacuation procedures
controls.barrier_role: Remove personnel from exposure to ammonia vapor
controls.lod_basis: Procedural control to minimize personnel exposure following release detection

supporting_text: All other Millard employees evacuated the facility to a location upwind from the ammonia release

controls.name: Emergency shutdown (e-stop) button
controls.barrier_role: De-energize pumps, compressors and valves to stop ammonia circulation
controls.lod_basis: Emergency control to halt system operation and limit continued release during incident

supporting_text: The use of the emergency stop button (e-stop), located in the Millard control room, would have shut down the compressors and pumps, and de-energized valves

Because the employees attempted to isolate the release to avoid shutting down refrigeration for the entire facility, the release quantity was significantly greater than it would have been had they simply shut the system down
Millard identified that the e-stop button should have been activated earlier by the Plant Engineer in their internal investigation report of the incident

controls.name: Emergency response procedures and training
controls.barrier_role: Coordinate response to ammonia release and minimize exposure
controls.lod_basis: Procedural and organizational controls for incident response

supporting_text: Approximately 15 minutes after being notified of the release, the Millard Plant Manager called 9-1-1, triggering a response from the Mobile Fire Department (MFD)
The MFD called the Mobile County Emergency Management Agency and advised shelter-in-place for the surrounding community and the U.S. Coast Guard halted water traffic on the industrial canal

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
